planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; small viburnum is native to moist streambanks and wet flatwoods of the Southeast and tolerates periodic flooding better than most viburnums.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; small viburnum is an underused evergreen to semi-evergreen native of Florida and the Gulf Coastal Plain, valued for its small, glossy, spoon-shaped leaves, masses of tiny white flower clusters in early spring before most plants awaken, and the bright red to dark purple-black drupes that provide exceptional wildlife food in summer and fall. Unlike many large viburnums, small viburnum stays naturally compact, to 6–8 feet in cultivation, and responds well to pruning — it can be maintained as a low hedge or specimen shrub. Very drought tolerant once established despite its riparian origins. The cultivar 'Mrs. Schiller's Delight' stays under 3 feet with particularly glossy foliage. An excellent choice for shaded understory positions and moist areas in zone 8–10 landscapes.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained to moist, acidic, sandy or loamy soil; Carolina bayberry tolerates poor, infertile, wet, and acidic conditions where most shrubs fail. Cold-stratify seeds for 3 months before sowing.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, acidic, sandy or poor soil in full sun; Carolina bayberry is a southeastern native shrub closely related to but distinct from the wax myrtle (Morella cerifera) — it is more compact and cold-hardy, with narrower leaves and slightly smaller, waxy gray berries on the female plants. Like all bayberries, only female plants produce berries, and one male is needed per 4–6 females for berry set. The fragrant, waxy berries were historically used to make bayberry candles, soap, and as a flavoring. The aromatic foliage is evergreen to semi-evergreen, releasing a pleasant spicy-resinous scent when crushed. Excellent for dry, infertile, sandy, acidic sites, roadsides, and coastal landscapes in zones 5–9 where it fixes nitrogen through root associations and requires virtually no care once established.

planting.note: Plant runners or potted plants in spring in well-drained, sandy, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; beach strawberry is naturally adapted to the dry, salty, sandy soils of coastal bluffs and beaches. Avoid rich soil or heavy fertilization, which produces excessive leafy growth and fewer fruits.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, infertile to average soil in full sun to light shade; beach strawberry is one of the two wild strawberry species that were crossed by French botanists in the 18th century to create the modern garden strawberry (Fragaria × ananassa) — the other parent being the Virginian strawberry (F. virginiana). It is native to the Pacific coast of the Americas from Alaska to Chile, naturally growing on sandy bluffs, dunes, and coastal prairies where it tolerates salt spray, wind, and poor soil. The fruits are small by commercial standards but deeply aromatic and richly flavored compared to grocery store strawberries. The leathery, blue-green leaves are distinctly more salt- and drought-tolerant than those of garden strawberries. An excellent low-maintenance groundcover for sunny, dry, or coastal sites; spreads by runners to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat.

planting.note: Plant runners or divisions in spring in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; musk strawberry is unique among strawberries in performing well in dappled or partial shade — a valuable trait for woodland or shaded garden situations where other strawberries fruit poorly.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; musk strawberry is a European native species and one of the most historically important cultivated strawberries before the modern hybrid garden strawberry (Fragaria × ananassa) replaced it in the 19th century. The medium-sized fruits have an intense, musky-aromatic fragrance far more complex and perfumed than modern strawberry varieties — often described as combining strawberry with pineapple and rose. The plant uniquely tolerates partial shade, making it one of the few berry crops for understory or semi-shaded settings. Most cultivated plants are of a single sex (plants were traditionally vegetatively propagated), so both male and female plants must be obtained for fruiting. 'Capron Royal' and 'Profumata di Tortona' are heritage Italian cultivars. The berries are soft and must be eaten immediately after harvest.

planting.note: Plant runners or divisions in spring in moist, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun; silverweed spreads aggressively by runners in moist, open sites and should be contained in garden settings. It naturally colonizes coastal beaches, estuary margins, and disturbed moist ground.

directions: Plant in moist, sandy or gravelly, open soil in full sun along coastal margins, wet meadows, and disturbed open ground; silverweed is a cosmopolitan coastal and temperate wildflower with distinctive pinnate leaves that are silver-silky on the underside — providing a striking two-toned effect as the wind turns the leaves. The bright yellow five-petaled flowers in summer attract numerous small native bees. The starchy taproots were historically an important famine food in coastal Scotland, Ireland, and in Indigenous communities across the Pacific Northwest, where they were boiled, roasted, or dried for winter storage. The roasted roots have a pleasant, slightly sweet, parsnip-like flavor. Spreads rapidly by runners and can become a weed in garden settings — best planted where it can naturalize on moist, open disturbed ground. Also listed as Argentina anserina in some taxonomic systems.
